questions.question: Explain the primary purpose of CPU virtualization and name the key hardware feature that enables its efficient implementation in modern processors.
keyPoints: The primary purpose is to present the illusion of multiple, isolated CPUs to different virtual machines (VMs) on a single physical machine.
It allows multiple operating systems to run concurrently on a single host, sharing the physical CPU resources.
The key hardware feature is the CPU's Virtualization Extension (e.g., Intel VT-x or AMD-V), which provides instructions to help the hypervisor trap and manage privileged operations from VMs.

questions.question: Describe the process of memory virtualization and explain the role of the hypervisor in managing the physical memory resources for multiple virtual machines.
keyPoints: Memory virtualization abstracts the physical RAM of the host machine, providing each VM with its own contiguous, zero-based physical memory space.
The hypervisor maintains a shadow page table or uses hardware-assisted virtualization (like Intel EPT or AMD RVI) to map the VM's 'physical' addresses to the actual machine addresses.
It is responsible for memory allocation, isolation between VMs, and efficient sharing of resources through techniques like memory overcommitment and ballooning.

questions.question: Discuss the main challenges in I/O device virtualization and compare the performance and isolation trade-offs between the three primary techniques: emulation, paravirtualization, and direct I/O passthrough.
keyPoints: The main challenges include performance overhead, achieving isolation between VMs, and complexity in managing shared access to physical devices.
Emulation (full virtualization) offers the best hardware compatibility and isolation but has the highest performance overhead due to trap-and-emulate operations in the hypervisor.
Paravirtualization uses modified guest drivers to communicate directly with the hypervisor, reducing overhead but requiring guest OS support and sacrificing some isolation.

Direct I/O passthrough (e.g., SR-IOV) offers near-native performance by granting a VM direct access to a physical device or a virtual function of it, but it can complicate live migration and reduce the flexibility of resource sharing.
